Summary: This study explores the performance of LI-RADS and radiomics from multiparametric MRI in predicting microvascular invasion (MVI) preoperatively in patients with solitary hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) < 5 cm. The study found that incomplete tumor capsule and corona enhancement on preoperative MRI were significantly related to MVI in solitary HCC < 5 cm and that multiple-sequence radiomic features potentially improve MVI-prediction-model performance.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to radiomics or machine learning-specific checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study clearly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient enrollment, which helps to establish a representative study population.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is histopathology, which is a high-quality reference standard for MVI.

Item4.reason: The study mentions that the data was collected at 'our hospital', indicating a single-center study.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is CE-MRI, which is a clinically translatable imaging technique for HCC diagnosis and staging.

Item6.reason: The study provides detailed imaging parameters for the MRI acquisition, including TR, TE, flip angle, FOV, matrix, section thickness, and gap.

Item7.reason: The study mentions that CE-MRI examination was performed within 30 days preoperatively, which is an appropriate interval to validate the presence or absence of MVI at the moment of diagnostic imaging exams.

Condition1.reason: The study includes tumor segmentation as part of the radiomic analysis workflow.

Condition2.reason: The study mentions that the ROI was drawn manually by two radiologists, indicating that the segmentation was not fully automated.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, mentioning that the ROI was drawn along the tumor margin layer by layer to generate a 3D-ROI by two radiologists.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The test set segmentation masks were produced by two radiologists, not a single reader or automated tool.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomic features using PyRadiomics, indicating the use of hand-crafted feature extraction.

Item11.reason: The study mentions preprocessing steps such as resampling the voxel size and gray-level intensity discretization, which are appropriate image preprocessing techniques.

Item12.reason: The study used PyRadiomics v.2.1.2 for feature extraction, which is a standardized feature extraction software.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (first-order statistics, morphological features, GLCM, GLSZM, GLRLM, NGTDM, and GLDM) and mentions that the same series of radiomic features were extracted from T2WI/FS, AP, PVP, and DP images.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomic features extracted from the images.

Condition5.reason: The study uses AdaBoost with hand-crafted radiomic features, not an end-to-end deep learning pipeline.

Item14.reason: The study employs ICC analysis to assess the reliability and stability of each feature and retains only those features exhibiting ICC values greater than 0.75.

Item15.reason: The study uses Pearson’s correlation to remove redundant features with mutual correlation coefficients > 0.9 and LASSO for feature selection.

Item16.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size or provide statistical evidence to support it.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item19.reason: The study does not explicitly address the handling of confounding factors.
